6 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I counted the operators in our shared state service last year. Forty-one. combineLatest, switchMap, distinctUntilChanged, shareReplay, stacked three and four deep, just to keep a sidebar toggle and a filter dropdown in sync with each other.
Forty-one operators, for state that never once needed to stream, race, or cancel anything. It just needed to exist, and be read, and be changed.
I rebuilt that same service with signals over one weekend. Nine lines did what forty-one operators used to do, and nobody on the team has filed a single bug against it since.
This is not about abandoning RxJS. It is about noticing how much of what we call reactive state was never actually a stream in the first place. Three patterns, three real rebuilds, and the numbers from each one.
Pattern 1: A Single Signal Object Instead of a Pile of Separate Subjects
The problem
UI state, a sidebar open flag, an active tab, a filter string, a sort order, usually starts as one BehaviorSubject and slowly grows into four or five, each one manually kept in sync with the others.
@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })
export class DashboardState {
private sidebarSubject = new…
